NA Patient was not discharged from an inpatient facility
Primary Reason for Home Health/Medical Necessity (POC Element):
James Helms, a 90-year-old male, is being referred for home health care following an encounter for change or removal of a non-surgical wound dressing, with a chronic ulcer on another part of
the left foot with the fat layer exposed; he recently underwent a revascularization procedure due to significant Peripheral Artery Disease and has a left second toe ulceration at risk for infection
requiring skilled wound care, with additional conditions including cellulitis of the left foot and a blister on the left foot, and following vascular surgery he continues to experience pain and
significant swelling in his lower extremities, indicating compromised circulation and the need for ongoing monitoring and skilled nursing care.
James's medical status reveals that he is alert and oriented but faces functional limitations due to pain and swelling, which hinders his mobility and daily activities. He requires skilled nursing
care for the management of his complex wound care regimen, including dressing changes that need to occur every other day, as well as education on recognizing signs of infection. Given his
age and the potential risks associated with his conditions, the skilled services provided by home health, including nursing, wound care, and possibly physical therapy, are medically necessary to
facilitate his recovery and prevent further complications.
Additionally, James's recent discharge from a vascular procedure underscores the urgency of home health admission, as his clinical needs must be addressed in a timely manner to promote
healing. The comprehensive care plan will focus on wound management, infection prevention, and enhancing his overall health status through skilled interventions, ensuring that he receives the
appropriate care in the comfort of his home. Therefore, the combination of his medical history, functional limitations, and the skilled care required to manage his conditions justify the need for
Criteria One (One must be met): Describe what assistive devices the patient is dependent on (if any) and specifically why the patient condition warrants the special transportation or the assistance
of another person or describe the condition that makes leaving home medically contraindicated.
Criteria 1 A: Because of illness or injury
Patient needs the aid of supportive devices
Patient Needs the use of special transportation.
Patient needs the assistance of another person to leave the place of residence
Criteria 1 B: Has a condition such as leaving his or her home is medically contraindicated.

List each diagnosis for which the patient is receiving home care in Column 1, and enter its ICD-10-CM code at the level of highest specificity in Column 2 (diagnosis codes only - no surgical or
procedure codes allowed). Diagnoses are listed in the order that best reflects the seriousness of each condition and supports the disciplines and services provided. Rate the degree of symptom
control for each condition in Column 2. ICD-10-CM sequencing requirements must be followed if multiple coding is indicated for any diagnoses.
Code each row according to the following directions for each column:
Column 1: Enter the description of the diagnosis. Sequencing of diagnoses should reflect the seriousness of each condition and support the disciplines and services provided. Column 2: Enter
the ICD-10-CM code for the condition described in Column 1- no surgical or procedure codes allowed. Codes must be entered at the level of highest specificity and ICD-10-CM coding rules and
sequencing requirements must be followed. Note that external cause codes (ICD-10-CM codes beginning with V, W, X, or Y) may not be reported in M1021 (Primary Diagnosis) but may be
reported in M1023 (Secondary Diagnoses). Also note that when a Z-code is reported in
Column 2 the code for the underlying condition can often be entered in Column 2, as long as it is an active on-going condition impacting home health care. Rate the degree of symptom control
for the condition listed in Column 1. Do not assign a symptom control rating if the diagnosis code is a V, W, X, Y or Z-code. Choose one value that represents the degree of symptom control
appropriate for each diagnosis using the following scale: 0 Asymptomatic, no treatment needed at this time 1 Symptoms well controlled with current therapy 2- Symptoms controlled with
difficulty, affecting daily functioning; patient needs ongoing monitoring 3- Symptoms poorly controlled; patient needs frequent adjustment in treatment and dose monitoring 4- Symptoms poorly
controlled; history of re-hospitalizations Note that the rating for symptom control in Column 2 should not be used to determine the sequencing of the diagnoses listed in Column 1. These are
separate items and sequencing may not coincide.

Patient's current ability to prepare and take all oral medications reliably and safely, including administration of the correct dosage at the appropriate times/intervals. Excludes injectable and IV medicat
ions. (NOTE: This refers to ability, not compliance or willingness.)
0. Able to independently take the correct oral medication(s) and proper dosage(s) at the correct times.
1. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if: (a) individual dosages are prepared in advance by another person; OR (b) another person develops a drug diary or chart.
2. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if given reminders by another person at the appropriate times.
3. Unable to take medication unless administered by another person

Patient's current ability to prepare and take all prescribed injectable medications reliably and safely, including administration of correct dosage at the appropriate times/intervals. Excludes IV medicatio

2. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if given reminders by another person based on the frequency of the injection
3. Unable to take injectable medication unless administered by another person

(M1740) Cognitive, behavioral, and psychiatric symptoms that are demonstrated at least once a week (Reported or Observed):
1. Memory deficit: failure to recognize familiar persons/places, inability to recall events of past 24 hours, significant memory loss so that supervision is required
2. Impaired decision-making: failure to perform usual ADLs or IADLs, inability to appropriately stop activities, jeopardizes safety through actions
3. Verbal disruption: yelling, threatening, excessive profanity, sexual references, etc.
4. Physical aggression: aggressive or combative to self and others (for example, hits self, throws objects, punches, dangerous maneuvers with wheelchair or other objects)
5. Disruptive, infantile, or socially inappropriate behavior (excludes verbal actions)
7. None of the above behaviors demonstrated

Patient's primary source of emotional support:
Ability to see in adequate light (with glasses or other visual appliances)
0. Adequate - sees fine detail, such as regular print in newspapers/books
1. Impaired - sees large print, but not regular print in newspapers/books
2. Moderate impaired - limited vision; not able to see newspaper headlines but can identify objects
3. Highly impaired - object identification in question, but eyes appear to follow objects
4. Severely impaired - no vision or sees only light, colors or shapes; eyes do not appear to follow objects
Ability to hear (with hearing aid or hearing appliances if normally used)
0. Adequate - no difficulty in normal conversation, social interaction, listening to TV
1. Minimal difficulty - difficulty in some environments (e.g., when person speaks softly, or setting is noisy)
2. Moderate difficulty - speaker has to increase volume and speak distinctly
3. Highly impaired - absence of useful hearing
(M1400) When is the patient dyspneic or noticeably Short of Breath?
0. Patient is not short of breath
1. When walking more than 20 feet, climbing stairs
2. With moderate exertion (for example, while dressing, using commode or bedpan, walking distances less than 20 feet)
3. With minimal exertion (for example, while eating, talking, or performing other ADLs) or with agitation

Ulcer/Injury that is Stageable at DC]
(M1311) Current Number of Unhealed Pressure Ulcers/Injuries at Each Stage:
A1. Stage 2: Partial thickness loss of dermis presenting as a shallow open ulcer with a red or pink wound bed, without slough. May also present as an intact
or open/ruptured blister. Number of Stage 2 pressure ulcers
B1. Stage 3: Full thickness tissue loss. Subcutaneous fat may be visible but bone, tendon, or muscle is not exposed. Slough may be present but does not
obscure the depth of tissue loss. May include undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 3 pressure ulcers
C1. Stage 4: Full thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. Slough or eschar may be present on some parts of the wound bed. Often
includes undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 4 pressure ulcers
D1. Unstageable: Non-removable dressing/device: Known but not stageable due to non removable dressing/device Number of unstageable pressure
E1. Unstageable: Slough and/or eschar: Known but not stageable due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar Number of unstageable
pressure ulcers due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar
F1. Unstageable: Deep tissue injury Number of unstageable pressure injuries presenting as deep tissue injury
(M1322) Current Number of Stage 1 Pressure Injuries:
Intact skin with non blanchable redness of a localized area usually over a bony prominence. Darkly pigmented skin may not have a visible blanching; in dark skin tones only it may appear with persis
(M1324) Stage of Most Problematic Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury that is Stageable:
(Excludes pressure ulcer/injury that cannot be staged due to a non removable dressing/device, coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar, or deep tissue injury.)
Stage of Most Problematic: Patient has no pressure ulcers or no stageable pressure ulcers

Code the patient's usual performance at SOC/ROC for each activity using the 6-point scale. If activity was not attempted at SOC/ROC, code the reason.
Safety and Quality of Performance - If helper assistance is required because patient's performance is unsafe or of poor quality, score according to amount of assistance provided.
Activities may be completed with or without assistive devices.
06. Independent - Patient completes the activity by him/herself with no assistance from a helper.
05. Setup or clean-up assistance - Helper sets up or cleans up; patient completes activity. Helper assists only prior to or following the activity.
Helper provides verbal cues and/or touching/steadying and/or contact guard assistance as patient completes activity. Assistance may be provided throughout the activity or intermittently.
03. Partial/moderate assistance - Helper does LESS THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts, holds or supports trunk or limbs, but provides less than half the effort.
02. Substantial/maximal assistance - Helper does MORE THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts or holds trunk or limbs and provides more than half the effort.
01. Dependent - Helper does ALL of the effort. Patient does none of the effort to complete the activity. Or, the assistance of 2 or more helpers is required for the patient to complete the activity.
If activity was not attempted, code reason:
09. Not applicable - Not attempted and the patient did not perform this activity prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
10. Not attempted due to environmental limitations (e.g., lack of equipment, weather constraints)

PLAN OF CARE ORDERS AND DISCHARGE PLAN
Orders and Treatment (Specify Frequency, Duration and Purpose of Visits): 484.60 (2.iv)
ADMIT PATIENT TO A PLUS HOME HEALTH SYSTEMS LLC SERVICES. ALL DIAGNOSIS AND MEDICATIONS VERIFIED WITH THE PRIMARY CARE PHYSICIAN.
James Helms, a 90-year-old male, is being referred for home health care following an encounter for change or removal of a non-surgical wound dressing, with a chronic ulcer on another part of
the left foot with the fat layer exposed; he recently underwent a revascularization procedure due to significant Peripheral Artery Disease and has a left second toe ulceration at risk for infection
requiring skilled wound care, with additional conditions including cellulitis of the left foot and a blister on the left foot, and following vascular surgery he continues to experience pain and
significant swelling in his lower extremities, indicating compromised circulation and the need for ongoing monitoring and skilled nursing care.
Skilled Nurse Visit Frequency: 1 time a week for 1 week, 2 times a week for 4 weeks for skilled observation and assessment, medication review and reconciliation, pain management, wound
assessment and wound care infection control measures, compliance with treatment regimen and therapeutic diet, home safety and disease process teaching.
Physical Therapy Visit Frequency: Evaluation and treatment for mobility, gait, transfer, established therapeutic exercises, strengthening, home safety and fall prevention techniques training.
HHA Visit Frequency: 2 times per week for 2 weeks, 1 time a week for 2 weeks for ADLs/personal care assistance effective on week of 04/12/26.
Wound #1: Venous Stasis Ulcer on dorsal aspect left foot under aseptic technique, SN to cleanse with wound cleanser, pat dry, apply xeroform, wrap with kerlix, secure with tape. Change
dressing every 2 to 3x weekly and as needed when soiled or dislodged. SN to instruct PT/CG to perform wound dressing change during non-SN visit days.
Wound #2: Venous Stasis Ulcer on 2nd left toe under aseptic technique, SN to cleanse with wound cleanser, pat dry, apply xeroform, wrap with kerlix, secure with tape. Change dressing every
2 to 3x weekly and as needed when soiled or dislodged. SN to instruct PT/CG to perform wound dressing change during non-SN visit days.
Wound #3: Venous Stasis Ulcer on medial aspect left ankle under aseptic technique, SN to cleanse with wound cleanser, pat dry, apply xeroform, wrap with kerlix, secure with tape. Change
dressing every 2 to 3x weekly and as needed when soiled or dislodged. SN to instruct PT/CG to perform wound dressing change during non-SN visit days.
Patient goals: The Patient aims to improve overall health, functional ability, independence, and understanding of self-care management. Patient also wishes wound to heal without any infection
and be free from pain. edema and signs and symptoms of disease exacerbation, as well as avoid falls and injuries.
Patient Covid 19 screening completed prior to visit. No report of symptoms or known COVID-19 exposure per patient/family. Patient provided with mask for use during HH visit. Patient agreed to use
mask during treatment. Clinician performed hand hygiene, donned mask, face shield and gloves and followed infection control and bag technique procedures during the visit. No COVID related
concerns during the time of visit. Disinfection of equipment followed including dry time. Proper disposal and doffing is done upon completion of the visit.

Explained Home Health service to patient/family/caregiver(s). Explained consent to care, release of liability, and patient rights & responsibilities. Consent to care, a liability release, and consent to
photograph were signed by the patient/responsible party. A comprehensive assessment of all body systems with OASIS elements evaluation was initiated. Reconciled facility discharge medications
against routine home prescriptions without issue. Discussed advance directives, privacy policy, COVID-19 prevention, 911 protocols, basic home safety, and fall prevention principles with
patient/family/caregiver(s). Discussed the plan of care draft with the patient and explained what to expect in the next visits and the remainder of the care episode. Also explained that the initial
discussion on the management of other disease processes will be done on the next visit.

SN assessed the patient's venous stasis ulcers located on the dorsal aspect of the left foot, 2nd left toe, and medial aspect of the left ankle, noting wound condition, drainage, and surrounding skin for
any signs of infection or deterioration. SN performed wound care using aseptic technique, cleansed the areas with wound cleanser, gently patted them dry, applied xeroform to maintain a moist
healing environment, and wrapped with kerlix secured with tape. SN actively monitored for redness, swelling, odor, or increased drainage and reinforced the importance of proper circulation, including
elevating the affected limb when possible. SN taught the patient/caregiver on proper wound care techniques, infection prevention, and signs to report promptly. Dressing changes were completed as
ordered, to be done 2-3 times weekly and as needed if soiled or dislodged. SN also instructed the patient/caregiver to perform wound care during non-visit days and ensured understanding through
SN assessed the patient with +2 pitting edema on bilateral lower extremities and cellulitis on the left lower extremity, noting increased swelling, skin warmth, redness, and tenderness. SN monitored
for any signs of worsening condition such as increased pain, spreading redness, or fever. SN elevated the lower extremities to help reduce swelling and promote venous return and encouraged the
patient to avoid prolonged standing or sitting without movement. SN reinforced the importance of proper skin hygiene and keeping the affected area clean and dry. SN also instructed on measures to
reduce edema, including leg elevation, gentle range of motion, and use of compression as tolerated and as ordered. SN taught the patient/caregiver on recognizing early signs of complications and
when to report changes to the healthcare provider and ensured understanding through verbal feedback.
SN assessed non-productive cough (frequency, duration, associated symptoms) and evaluated lung sounds/respiratory status. Encouraged fluids and humidified air, proper upright positioning, and
avoidance of irritants. Reinforced adherence to prescribed treatments. Instructed patient/caregiver to monitor and report worsening symptoms (productive cough, fever, dyspnea); understanding
confirmed. SN assessed bleeding precautions related to anticoagulant use, monitoring for signs of bleeding (bruising, petechiae, gum/nose bleeding, hematuria, blood in stool) and checked vital

signs for instability. Reinforced safety measures including soft toothbrush, electric razor, fall/injury prevention, and avoiding sharp objects and alcohol. Instructed medication adherence without dose
changes. Taught patient/caregiver on warning signs and when to seek urgent care; understanding confirmed.
SN assessed the patient reporting 5/10 pain on the dorsal aspect of the left foot, noting location, intensity, and any factors that aggravated or relieved the discomfort. SN assessed for associated
symptoms such as redness, swelling, or limited mobility. SN encouraged the patient to elevate the affected extremity and avoid excessive pressure or prolonged standing to help reduce discomfort.
SN reinforced non-pharmacological pain management techniques such as rest, proper positioning, and use of supportive footwear. SN also instructed the patient to follow prescribed pain
management measures as directed and to report any increase in pain or new symptoms. SN taught the patient/caregiver to monitor pain levels regularly and to notify the healthcare provider if pain
worsens or becomes unrelieved, ensuring understanding through verbal feedback.
SN assessed patient with generalized muscle weakness and high fall risk, evaluating gait, balance, and mobility status. SN ensured fall precautions in place, including safe environment, clutter-free
surroundings, proper lighting, and assistive device use as needed. SN reinforced need for assistance with ambulation and transfers. SN encouraged energy conservation and gradual activity as
tolerated. SN instructed patient/caregiver on fall prevention strategies and to report any dizziness, weakness, or near-fall incidents; understanding confirmed via verbal feedback.
SOC, POC, Visit frequencies and update of patient health condition, wound care consult, shower assist, PT eval
SOC, POC, Visit frequencies and update of patient health condition, wound care consult, shower assist, PT eval
SN will continue to assess the patient's overall health condition, wound assessment and wound care patient/PCG understanding of the disease process, functional status, and knowledge of self-care

Ongoing education and training provided by the SN, as appropriate, regarding the care and services identified in the plan of care.
Able to understand medication regime and care related to diagnoses.
Discharge when medical condition is stable and patient is no longer in need of skilled services.
Discharge when goals met. Discharge when wound(s) healed.

To self care when max potential achieved
To outpatient therapy with MD approval
James Helms, a 90-year-old male, seen for wound care of a chronic toe ulcer and a new, recurrent blister on the dorsal left foot with drainage. His history includes an artificial heart valve, atrial
fibrillation, recent left leg vascular surgery with stent, and anticoagulation with Warfarin and aspirin. Assessment revealed multiple non-healing wounds, elevated glucose, and pressure points
from footwear. Plan included disconstricting sandals, wound care, PT for fall risk, home health for showering, and smoking cessation counseling.
Toe pain and blister on top of foot that comes and goes and leaks
James Helms is a 90-year-old male with a history of artificial heart valve replacement, recent vascular surgery with stent placement, and anticoagulation therapy who presents for wound care
management of chronic foot ulcerations and blistering.
The patient has been experiencing issues with his toe for an extended period, with the caregiver noting this has been the "main thing." Additionally, he has developed a blister on the top of his
foot that intermittently appears, disappears, and leaks fluid. The toe issue has been present for a long time, while the foot blister developed more recently, with the patient being taken to a
podiatrist on March 27th after the blister started small and progressively enlarged over a couple of days.
The patient reports pain in the toe area and describes experiencing pain "a little bit" in various locations on his foot, including between toes, where the blister is located, and on the back of his

foot. He also mentions that his "little one has been bothering me lately," referring to his little toe. The pain is described as intermittent, with the patient stating "I get pain" but noting it's "not there
Recent healthcare interactions include an urgent care visit where a small sore on his toe was identified. Following this, he had a follow-up appointment with his primary care provider while his
caregiver was out of town. The primary care provider diagnosed him with "a raging case of athlete's foot" and prescribed antifungal medication without physically examining his feet, according to
the caregiver. When his foot subsequently swelled significantly, he was given diuretics. A subsequent podiatrist evaluation contradicted the athlete's foot diagnosis and led to vascular surgery
The patient underwent vascular surgery on March 31st for arterial revascularization with stent placement in his left leg, with plans to discuss intervention for the other leg at an upcoming follow-
up appointment. He is currently on anticoagulation therapy with Coumadin, with INR monitoring every two weeks, and his most recent INR was 2.4 as of Monday.
The patient's current footwear consists of sandals that were purchased when he was initially diagnosed with athlete's foot, though he typically wears socks and tennis shoes. His caregiver has
been managing wound care at home using petroleum-based products, gauze, and tape as directed, with dressing changes performed most recently on Thursday night.
Peripheral arterial disease with angioplasty and stent placement to left leg on March 31, 2026
Memory issues described as "hit and miss"
Heart valve replacement with metal valve (artificial valve)
Angioplasty and stent placement in left leg on March 31st
Warfarin (Coumadin) 2.5 mg and 5 mg by mouth
Takes 5 mg four days a week and 2.5 mg three days a week
- Followed by Dr. Andrew Ayers with labs every 2 weeks
Most recent INR was 2.4 as of Monday
Given by primary care for presumed athlete's foot
Substance Use: Current smoker (1 pack per day)
Living Situation: Lives at home with daughter who provides care and assistance
- Social Support: Daughter serves as primary caregiver and accompanies to medical appointments
Neurological: Positive for hit and miss memory issues.
General: 90-year-old male who appears stated age. Uses walker for ambulation.
Skin: Multiple foot lesions present. Large blister on dorsal aspect of left foot that comes and goes and leaks fluid. Chronic sore on toe. Additional sore on medial aspect of foot. Sore on posterior
ankle causing ankle pain. Skin breakdown noted at pressure points from sandal straps. Tan lines visible on feet consistent with sandal wear pattern.
Musculoskeletal: Ankle range of motion intact with dorsiflexion and plantarflexion. Foot flexibility maintained.
Neurological: Oriented to person, place, and time. Reports memory as "hit and miss." Feet noted to be sensitive to touch with areas of both pain and tickling sensation during examination.

James Helms is a 90-year-old male with history of artificial heart valve, recent vascular surgery, and anticoagulation therapy presenting for home health evaluation of chronic toe ulcer and new
foot blistering with concerns for poor wound healing.
Chronic toe ulcer with new foot blistering
Assessment: Patient has a longstanding ulcer on his toe that has been present since at least February 21st, initially misdiagnosed and treated as athlete's foot by primary care. New blistering
has developed on the dorsal aspect of his foot that comes and goes with drainage. Multiple pressure points and skin breakdown are noted on both feet, including sores on the inside of the foot
and back of ankle. The constellation of multiple non-healing wounds, elevated blood glucose levels, and poor circulation from smoking raises significant concern for diabetic ulceration, though
diabetes has not been formally diagnosed. Current footwear (tight sandals) is contributing to pressure points and preventing healing.
Discontinue current sandals due to constricting pressure on wounds
Provide non-slip socks for indoor use instead of shoes
Request wound care consultation for specialized assessment and management recommendations
Coordinate with existing podiatrist care (patient has follow-up scheduled for April 21st)
Obtain hemoglobin A1C to evaluate for diabetes given elevated glucose and poor wound healing
Patient education on proper foot care and importance of appropriate footwear
Assessment: Patient has had recent falls, including one "the other day" when going to the car where he caught himself. He uses a walker but admits to being "stubborn" and not consistently
using it. Current walker has brakes which may actually increase fall risk if patient cannot engage them quickly enough. Patient reports feeling lightheaded and having muscle weakness,
particularly concerning given his anticoagulation status and risk for serious injury from falls.
Order physical therapy evaluation for strengthening and balance assessment
Patient education on consistent walker use, especially given anticoagulation risk
Assessment: Patient showers infrequently (approximately once per week) and requires assistance due to mobility limitations and fall risk. Proper hygiene is critical for wound healing, and
patient experiences lightheadedness and difficulty with lower leg care during showering. Hot water can cause vasodilation leading to increased dizziness and fall risk.
Arrange home health aide for shower assistance 2-3 times per week until wound healing improves
Use shower chair consistently for safety
Keep affected foot dry during showering with protective covering
Patient education on importance of hygiene for wound healing
Assessment: Patient is on warfarin therapy following recent artificial valve replacement and vascular surgery on March 31st. Current regimen is 5mg four days per week and 2.5mg three days
per week. Most recent INR was 2.4 as of Monday, which is within therapeutic range. INR monitoring is being done every two weeks with full blood work.
Continue INR monitoring every two weeks
Follow-up with Dr. Andrew Ayers for anticoagulation management
Assessment: Patient smokes one pack per day, which significantly impairs circulation and wound healing by constricting blood vessels in the extremities. This is particularly concerning given his
current foot wounds and vascular disease.
Patient education on smoking's impact on wound healing and circulation
Stop wearing sandals immediately they are too tight and causing pressure sores
Wear non-slip socks instead of shoes when possible
Use loose, non-slip slippers for walking around the house
For going out, wear very loose regular socks and loose sneakers only when necessary
- Keep foot dry during showering do not soak the wound
- Use a shower chair when bathing
- Leave the foam cushion on your foot to protect the pressure point
- Use your walker consistently to prevent falls
Accept help with showering until foot heals
- Have someone present when showering due to dizziness risk
Physical therapy evaluation for balance and strength
Home health aide for shower assistance
